assumptions.assumption: Gift selection (knowing what to get) is the primary pain point for gifters
evidence: Pain research Phase 2: Anxiety scored 25, Ideation scored 16
Fear of judgment > not knowing what to get
assumptions.notes: UPDATED: Research showed anxiety/judgment fear is primary, not ideation

assumptions.assumption: Gifters would trust AI recommendations for gift selection
evidence: ADHD segment already pays for AI assistance ($79-130/mo)
No direct evidence of trust barrier yet

assumptions.assumption: Recipients want better gifts but don't want to share wishlists
evidence: Pain research: 'transactional feel' mentioned repeatedly
Wishlists feel grabby

assumptions.assumption: ADHD adults will pay $10-20/month for gift delegation
evidence: ADHD apps market $2.78B
Focusmate $79/mo, coaching $130/mo
High pain intensity for gifting
assumptions.test_method: Fake door test with pricing page

assumptions.assumption: 'Arrive ready' (wrapped, carded, on time) is high-value differentiator
evidence: 52% said wrapping worst part of holidays
71% would pay someone else to wrap

assumptions.assumption: Proactive reminders with reduced task complexity beats standard reminders
evidence: ADHD pain: reminders remind without reducing complexity
Need 'Here are 3 options' not just 'birthday in 2 weeks'

assumptions.assumption: Corporate/B2B segment has higher willingness to pay than personal gifting
evidence: ADHD personal WTP ($79-130/mo) rivals B2B
B2B is crowded/consolidating
assumptions.notes: UPDATED: ADHD segment shows comparable WTP to B2B

assumptions.assumption: Subscription model can achieve positive unit economics
evidence: Cost model: AI costs $0.02/recommendation
AI costs <1% of $10/mo subscription

assumptions.assumption: LLMs can generate meaningfully better gift suggestions than random selection
evidence: Empirical test Phase 4: 7/10 with context vs 3/10 cold start
Curated catalog improves further
assumptions.test_method: Blind test - AI vs random vs human

assumptions.assumption: AI can infer preferences without explicit profile data
evidence: Cold-start test: Generic 'Pinterest-like' results
Needs explicit input for quality
assumptions.notes: UPDATED: Cold start produces generic results. Need explicit input.

assumptions.assumption: 200-item curated catalog beats open-ended AI recommendations
evidence: AI feasibility research: constrained inventory improves quality
Jam study principle

assumptions.assumption: Giver-centric gift framing increases relationship closeness more than recipient-centric
evidence: Aknin & Human 2015: 6 studies show giver-centric gifts promote more closeness
Self-disclosure is intrinsically rewarding (Tamir & Mitchell 2012)
assumptions.test_method: A/B test: 'Share yourself' vs 'Match their preferences' framing
assumptions.notes: Counterintuitive finding - people prefer to GIVE recipient-centric but OUTCOMES are better with giver-centric

assumptions.assumption: Conversational turn-taking interface increases AI acceptance for close relationships
evidence: Fu et al 2024: Turn-taking AI increases expected preference matching
Mimics human conversation pattern
assumptions.test_method: Compare chatbot vs form-based interface adoption rates

assumptions.assumption: Users resist AI more for close relationships than distant ones
evidence: Fu et al 2024: 5 studies confirm social closeness negatively predicts AI tool use
Relational signaling motive dominates for close friends
assumptions.notes: Implication: Start with execution (lower stakes) not selection for close relationships

assumptions.assumption: Positioning AI as 'thinking partner' vs 'decision maker' increases adoption
evidence: AI aversion research: ownership/effort attribution matters
Ward & Broniarczyk 2016: effort = value in close relationships
assumptions.test_method: Messaging A/B test on landing page

assumptions.assumption: Givers overweight monetary value while recipients prefer practical/useful gifts
evidence: Baskin et al 2014: construal level differences
Givi et al 2021: relative vs absolute value mismatch
assumptions.test_method: Blind preference test - giver vs recipient ratings
